definition: Controlled experiment comparing two variants to determine which performs better against a specific metric or goal.
The gradual increase in asset value or earnings due to business growth, typically used in M&A to describe EPS improvement post-acquisition.
Accounting method recognizing revenue when earned and expenses when incurred, regardless of cash flow timing.
EBITDA modified to exclude non-recurring, irregular, and one-time items that may not reflect normal business operations, providing clearer operational performance view.
Average Daily Trading Volume
Anti-Money Laundering compliance framework and regulatory requirements designed to prevent financial crimes and illicit fund transfers.
Annual Percentage Rate
Annual Recurring Revenue, a key SaaS metric measuring predictable yearly revenue from subscriptions and contracts.
Efficiency ratio measuring how effectively a company uses its assets to generate revenue, calculated as revenue divided by average total assets.
Assets Under Management
One hundredth of one percentage point (0.01%), commonly used to express interest rate changes and yield spreads.
Standard or reference point against which performance, quality, or other metrics are measured and compared.
Net asset value of a company calculated as total assets minus total liabilities, representing shareholder equity on the balance sheet.
Short-term funding used to cover immediate cash needs until permanent financing is secured or a specific event occurs.
Compound Annual Growth Rate, measuring the mean annual growth rate of an investment over a specified time period longer than one year.
Capitalization rate used in real estate to estimate potential return on investment, calculated as net operating income divided by property value.
Capital Expenditure, funds used to acquire, upgrade, or maintain physical assets such as property, equipment, or infrastructure.
Corporate restructuring where a parent company sells a minority stake in a subsidiary through an IPO while retaining controlling interest.
Metric measuring the time between paying for inventory and receiving cash from sales, indicating operational efficiency and liquidity management.
Net amount of cash being transferred into and out of a business during a specific period.
Cost of Goods Sold, direct costs attributable to the production of goods sold by a company, including materials and direct labor.
Asset pledged as security for a loan, which the lender can seize if the borrower defaults on payment obligations.
Financial statements prepared as of the completion date to determine final purchase price adjustments based on actual working capital and debt levels.
Binding commitment in a loan agreement requiring the borrower to meet certain financial ratios or operational requirements.
Customer Relationship Management
Liquidity ratio measuring a company's ability to pay short-term obligations, calculated as current assets divided by current liabilities.
Discounted Cash Flow, valuation method estimating investment value based on projected future cash flows adjusted for time value of money.
Cash required to cover repayment of interest and principal on debt obligations for a particular period.
Advance payments received for goods or services to be delivered in the future, recorded as a liability until earned.
Reduction in existing shareholders' ownership percentage due to issuance of new shares or conversion of convertible securities.
Strategic decision to sell, liquidate, or spin off a business unit, subsidiary, or asset portfolio.
Days Payable Outstanding, average number of days a company takes to pay its suppliers and vendors.
Days Sales Outstanding, average number of days to collect payment after a sale, indicating accounts receivable efficiency.
Comprehensive investigation and analysis of a business or investment opportunity to verify facts, assess risks, and validate assumptions.
Contractual provision where seller receives additional future compensation based on the business achieving certain post-acquisition financial targets.
Earnings Before Interest and Taxes, measuring operating profitability before financing costs and tax obligations.
Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization, a proxy for operating cash flow used in valuation.
Total company value including market cap plus debt minus cash, representing the theoretical takeover price of a business.
Earnings Per Share, portion of company profit allocated to each outstanding share of common stock.
Market value of a company's shares, representing the residual claim on assets after all liabilities are satisfied.
Enterprise Resource Planning, integrated software systems managing core business processes across finance, HR, and operations.
Employee Stock Ownership Plan, benefit plan providing workers with ownership stake in the company through allocated shares.
Economic Value Added, measure of financial performance calculating the value created beyond required return on capital.
Valuation multiple assumed at the end of a projection period to estimate terminal value in DCF or LBO models.
Free Cash Flow, cash generated by operations after capital expenditures, available for distribution to investors.
First In, First Out inventory valuation method assuming oldest inventory items are sold first.
Fair Market Value, price at which an asset would trade between willing buyer and seller in arm's length transaction.
Full-Time Equivalent, unit measuring workload equal to one full-time employee's hours.
Generally Accepted Accounting Principles, standard framework of accounting rules and guidelines in the United States.
Intangible asset representing the premium paid over fair value of net identifiable assets in an acquisition.
Gross Profit, revenue minus cost of goods sold, indicating profitability before operating expenses.
Percentage of revenue remaining after subtracting cost of goods sold, measuring production efficiency and pricing power.
Minimum acceptable rate of return on an investment, used as a threshold for capital allocation decisions.
International Financial Reporting Standards, global accounting framework used in most countries outside the United States.
Reduction in recoverable value of an asset below its carrying amount on the balance sheet, requiring a write-down.
Initial Public Offering, first sale of company stock to the public, transitioning from private to public ownership.
Internal Rate of Return, discount rate making net present value of all cash flows equal to zero.
Key Performance Indicator, quantifiable measure used to evaluate success in achieving business objectives.
Use of borrowed capital to increase potential return on investment, measured by debt-to-equity or similar ratios.
Last In, First Out inventory valuation method assuming most recently acquired items are sold first.
Ability to convert assets to cash quickly without significant loss in value, or availability of liquid assets.
Deal mechanism where purchase price is fixed based on a balance sheet at specified date prior to closing with no post-closing adjustment.
Letter of Intent, preliminary agreement outlining terms of a proposed transaction before definitive documentation.
Last Twelve Months, financial data from the most recent rolling twelve-month period, also called TTM.
Mergers and Acquisitions, corporate transactions combining companies or transferring ownership of business entities.
Material Adverse Change, significant negative event allowing a party to terminate a transaction under certain conditions.
Multiple on Invested Capital, ratio of total value to invested capital, measuring investment return in private equity.
Monthly Recurring Revenue, predictable monthly revenue from subscriptions, a key metric for SaaS businesses.
Net Asset Value, total assets minus total liabilities, commonly used to value investment funds.
Non-Disclosure Agreement, legal contract protecting confidential information shared between parties.
Total debt minus cash and cash equivalents, representing the actual debt burden if all liquid assets were used to pay down obligations.
Net Operating Income, revenue minus operating expenses excluding interest and taxes, commonly used in real estate.
Contractual agreement restricting a party from engaging in competing business activities for a specified period and geography.
Adjustments to financial statements removing non-recurring or unusual items to present sustainable operating performance.
Net Present Value, difference between present value of cash inflows and outflows, used to evaluate investment profitability.
Net Working Capital, current assets minus current liabilities, measuring short-term liquidity and operational efficiency.
Operating Expenditure, ongoing costs for running business operations excluding capital investments.
Profit and Loss Statement, financial report summarizing revenues, costs, and expenses during a specific period.
Price-to-Earnings ratio, valuation metric comparing stock price to earnings per share.
Price/Earnings to Growth ratio, valuation metric adjusting P/E for expected earnings growth rate.
Payment In Kind, debt instrument where interest is paid by issuing additional debt rather than cash.
Purchase Price Allocation, process of assigning acquisition cost to acquired assets and liabilities at fair value.
Financial projections or statements adjusted to reflect proposed transactions or hypothetical scenarios.
Quality of Earnings, analysis examining the sustainability, accuracy, and sources of reported earnings.
Liquidity measure calculated as liquid assets divided by current liabilities, excluding inventory from current assets.
Research and Development, activities directed toward innovation and introduction of new products or processes.
Representations and Warranties, statements of fact and promises in transaction documents subject to indemnification.
Accounting principle determining when and how revenue should be recorded in financial statements.
Return on Assets, profitability ratio measuring net income generated relative to total assets.
Return on Capital Employed, profitability ratio measuring how efficiently capital is used to generate profits.
Return on Equity, profitability measure calculated as net income divided by shareholders' equity.
Return on Investment, performance measure evaluating efficiency and profitability of an investment.
Annualized projection of financial results based on current period performance, assuming consistent conditions.
Software as a Service, cloud-based software delivery model with subscription-based pricing and hosted infrastructure.
Selling, General and Administrative expenses, operating costs not directly tied to production.
Ability of a company to meet its long-term debt obligations and continue operations indefinitely.
Sale and Purchase Agreement, definitive legal document governing terms and conditions of a business sale.
Additional value created by combining two companies, typically through cost savings or revenue enhancement.
Total Addressable Market, total revenue opportunity available for a product or service.
Estimated value of a business beyond the explicit forecast period in a DCF model.
Total Shareholder Return, investment return including capital gains and dividends over a specified period.
Trailing Twelve Months, financial data from the most recent twelve-month period, same as LTM.
Process of determining the economic value of a business, asset, or investment opportunity.
Weighted Average Cost of Capital, blended cost of debt and equity financing used as discount rate.
Current assets minus current liabilities, measuring short-term financial health and operational efficiency.
Accounting action removing an asset's value from the books, recognizing it as a loss or expense.
Year over Year, comparison of a metric between the current period and same period in the previous year.
Year to Date, cumulative period from the beginning of the current year through the present date.
